title: Azure Logic Apps - Concurrency Control, Rate-Limit Management, And Throttling Patterns
applies_to: Controlling parallel execution in Logic Apps foreach loops
Handling API rate limits from Qualys, BitSight, and Microsoft Graph
Managing concurrent workflow instances on shared triggers
Token refresh and credential expiry in long-running workflows

confirmed_facts.fact: For-Each loop concurrency: by default Logic Apps run foreach iterations in parallel (up to 20 concurrent for Consumption, 50 for Standard). Set a specific concurrency degree in the loop settings: in the designer, select the foreach action > Settings > Concurrency Control > On, set degree from 1 (sequential) to the desired value. Sequential processing (degree 1) is required when the downstream API enforces strict per-user rate limits.

Trigger concurrency: for recurrence and polling triggers, enable concurrency control to limit how many simultaneous workflow instances can run. In the trigger settings, set Concurrency Control > On > Degree. Default unlimited. Set to 1 to ensure only one pipeline instance runs at a time — important for daily security data pipelines that write to shared SharePoint lists.

Handling 429 Too Many Requests from external APIs: the HTTP action has a built-in retry policy. Set it to 'Exponential Interval' with Count=4, Interval=PT5S, Maximum=PT1H, Minimum=PT5S. Logic Apps will automatically retry on 408, 429, and 5xx responses using exponential backoff. Accessible via HTTP action Settings > Retry Policy.

Respect the Retry-After header from throttled APIs: after the HTTP action returns 429, use a Condition to check the header: `@outputs('HTTP_Action')['headers']['Retry-After']`. Then add a Delay action with duration set to that value before retrying via a scope-based loop. The built-in retry policy does not automatically parse Retry-After; custom handling is needed for APIs like Microsoft Graph that specify exact retry windows.

Logic Apps itself can be throttled by Azure when it exceeds action execution rate limits. Consumption: 100,000 actions per 5 minutes per region. Standard: governed by the App Service Plan SKU. Throttled runs appear in metrics as 'Runs Throttled'. Mitigation: use batching to reduce action count, or split large foreach loops across multiple workflow runs via a queue.

Token refresh for long-running workflows: OAuth access tokens expire (typically 1 hour for Entra ID tokens). If a workflow runs longer than token lifetime, subsequent HTTP calls fail with 401. Pattern: use a child workflow or inline token refresh action that calls the token endpoint again before each major API segment. Cache the new token in a workflow variable.

Queue-based debouncing for high-frequency triggers: instead of triggering directly on each event, route events to an Azure Service Bus queue and use a Logic App with a Service Bus trigger that processes items at a controlled rate. Set the trigger polling interval to manage throughput. This decouples the ingestion rate from the processing rate.

Managed connector connection throttling: managed connectors (SharePoint, Office 365) have per-connection call limits enforced by the connector gateway. SharePoint connector: 600 calls per 60 seconds per connection. If a foreach loop hits this, spread connections across multiple connection objects or reduce foreach concurrency to stay within limits.

implementation_notes: For daily Qualys/BitSight pipelines: set trigger concurrency to 1 to guarantee no overlapping runs. If the previous day's run is still executing when the next trigger fires, the new instance is queued (up to the waiting runs limit, default 10).

When iterating over a large array (e.g., 500 Qualys hosts) in a foreach, start with concurrency degree 5 and monitor throttling metrics. Increase only if 429s are absent and the external API supports it.

For Microsoft Graph calls within foreach loops, prefer Invoke-MgGraphRequest style or Graph batch requests (up to 20 per batch) rather than individual HTTP actions to stay within Graph's 10,000 requests per 10-minute throttle.

When building retry logic manually for APIs without standard Retry-After headers (e.g., Qualys), use a Do-Until loop with a condition on the HTTP status code and a Delay action with a fixed 60-second interval.
caveats: The built-in retry policy retries on transient failures but does NOT parse Retry-After header values. For APIs like Graph that specify exact retry windows, implement custom retry logic.

Setting foreach concurrency to 1 (sequential) on a large array can significantly increase total workflow duration. Evaluate whether the API rate limit actually requires sequential processing before applying.
Waiting runs for Consumption Logic Apps are limited to 10 by default. If the trigger fires more than 10 times while a run is in progress (e.g., catch-up after a failure), excess triggers are dropped.
